author: @sc-lj9cp
text: Don’t listen to the naysayers Rob. I watch you every day and marvel at your honesty, sense of humour and ingenuity. I wonder what would happen if yourself, Colin Furzell and Mat Armstrong and his Dad got together to work on something..

author: @Route72Trucks
text: We love what you’re doing Rob! Some people will just never understand no matter how hard you try.

author: @ile84
text: So what you basically said was that research is "trial and error, mostly error" like all science has been, for quite the long time. I have also noticed that this one thing I made as a heater the first lid I used in it was a let down since it had a hinge and basically flat piece of metal, sure it looked kind of ok, but I had to angle grind it away since I already welded it in place and had to make a new lid that is taken completely off and doesn't leak all the smoke inside, I remember pondering about that lid for very long time and in the end I just gave up and made it a new. Had also tried to use some silicone for the old lid, but the first time I put the fire on that silicone sealant just dried up and I thought that to hell with it. Sometimes the simpler solutions are the best, no need to over complicate things.

text:

author: @chrisstevens2
text: Extra thumbs for the kitty!

author: @chiperchap
text: Declining health has forced me to spend too much time on my keister Rob which torments me to no end! But honestly having you there to try all these new things and investigate them has not only kept me entertained and sane it also given me the chance to enjoy my limited energy to have a go and made some real progress in improving my situation both actually and mentally. In fact I'm sitting here on my keister enjoying free renewable electricity it front of a toasty methanol heater, showering in hot water for almost free and generally happier! I can't spend all day tinkering anymore but I can take what I've learned for your experiments and the comments section and actually do something! I can learn and get informed and gain ideas or simply knowledge and when i have a good day I'm in the shed and making something in a couple of hours! I don't think you could ever truly appreciate the influence uncle Rob has on me. Not to mention the inspiration to my kids! My interest in your videos hasn't only effected me but it's woken curiosity and science to them in a way I never could. (nobody listened to dad before haha)

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text:
I like the John Maxwell quote: "Fail early, fail often, but always fail forward!" which I take as referring to the importance of iteration. You can only find the optimum way to do something by trying all the ways it could possibly be done, or as many as you possibly can. It follows that unless you have regular failures then you can't really be innovating, you're only following well-trodden paths. As you've shown in many videos, there are the occasional gems left forgotten along old paths, but that's more like techno-archaeology.

Keep failing, Rob! ;-)

author: @cosmicseekin
text: An optimist like you should stick to your guns. More of your work has been inspiring than not. It would be helpful to know where the potential of your revelations come on a scale of a fizzle to an atomic bomb. Chasing micro watts and volts is acceptable but if there is no chance of beating present technology, we need to be honest with ourselves. Your videos are very welcome on winters days.

replies.author: @carmichaelmoritz8662
replies.text: If we chose devices that used less Its obvious that micro watts would prevail.  Using more of anything only truly benefits the greedy and wealthy.
